# Live Prediction

::: {.callout-note}
## Why this chapter matters

Live Prediction closes the loop between a real phone Sensor Stream and the trained Model. This chapter shows how all earlier components work together when data arrives continuously and the system has to respond in real time.
:::

## Learning Objectives

After completing this chapter, you should be able to

- explain how Live Prediction connects the physical world to the trained Model,
- configure phyphox remote access,
- explain why the phone and laptop must be on the same network,
- describe the live prediction pipeline,
- explain why Window size creates prediction delay,
- run the Streamlit dashboard,
- troubleshoot common live demo failures.

---

## Live Prediction Connects The Physical World To The Model

Until now, the project has mostly used stored data.

Live Prediction changes the situation.

A real phone moves in the physical world. phyphox streams the phone acceleration. The dashboard collects recent samples, creates a Window, extracts a Feature Vector, and asks the Model for a Prediction.

That is the complete system in motion.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  A[Smartphone] --> B[phyphox]
  B --> C[HTTP]
  C --> D[Buffer]
  D --> E[Window]
  E --> F[Features]
  F --> G[Model]
  G --> H[Dashboard]
```

The important lesson is that machine learning is now only one part of a live chain.

If any link fails, the demo fails.

---

## phyphox Setup

Install phyphox on your smartphone.

Open an acceleration experiment and enable remote access.

phyphox will show a URL such as:

```text
http://192.168.178.45:8080
```

Enter this URL in the Streamlit dashboard.

phyphox acts as the live sensor source.

It turns the smartphone accelerometer into an HTTP endpoint that the laptop can request.

::: {.callout-warning}
## Network Requirement

The phone and laptop must be able to reach each other on the local network.

University WiFi networks such as eduroam often block direct device-to-device communication.

If the laptop cannot open the phyphox URL in a browser, the dashboard cannot read sensor data either.
:::

This is why a hotspot often works better for class demos.

A phone hotspot usually places the phone and laptop on a small local network where they can talk directly.

Recommended network options:

- smartphone hotspot,
- local home WiFi,
- travel router,
- lab router configured for local device communication.

---

## Start the Dashboard

From the project root:

```bash
uv run streamlit run dashboard.py
```

This command starts a Streamlit app: a Python script that becomes an interactive browser dashboard for the live demo.

The dashboard lets you configure:

- phyphox URL,
- window size,
- demo duration.

The demo is intentionally time-limited so that it cannot run forever by accident.

---

## Live Prediction Pipeline

The live pipeline is:

```text
phyphox
→ PhyphoxClient
→ LiveBuffer
→ Window
→ Feature Vector
→ Model
→ Streamlit dashboard
```

`PhyphoxClient` reads the latest x, y and z acceleration values.

`LiveBuffer` stores the most recent samples until a full Window is available.

The Window is converted to the same Feature Vector used during training.

The prediction service loads the trained Random Forest Model and returns:

- predicted activity,
- confidence,
- class probabilities.

::: {.callout-tip}
## Engineering Insight

Live Prediction is where hidden assumptions become visible.

Sampling rate, network access, buffer size, feature order, and model artifacts all have to work at the same time.
:::

---

## Why Predictions React With Delay

The dashboard cannot classify one isolated acceleration sample.

It needs a full Window.

If the Window is five seconds long, the system needs about five seconds of recent Sensor Stream data before the first Prediction can appear.

Shorter Windows can react faster but may be noisier.

Longer Windows can be more stable but react more slowly.

This is why Live Prediction always includes a trade-off between responsiveness and stability.

---

## Dashboard States

The dashboard shows the current live demo state:

| State | Meaning |
|---|---|
| Waiting | The demo has not started yet |
| Collecting samples | The buffer is filling |
| Predicting | A full window is available |
| Error | phyphox or model prediction failed |
| Finished | The configured demo duration ended |

These states make live demos easier to debug in front of a class.

---

## Thinking Like An Engineer

Instead of asking:

> Does the Model work?

ask:

> Can the complete live system keep producing Predictions when real-world conditions change?

In a classroom demo, the most likely failures may be networking, phone setup, timing, or missing artifacts rather than machine learning accuracy.

---

## Troubleshooting

A live demo can fail for machine learning reasons, but it more often fails for ordinary system reasons.

Use the table before changing code.

| Problem | Likely Cause | Fix |
|---|---|---|
| phyphox URL does not open in the laptop browser | Wrong IP address or phone and laptop are on different networks | Copy the current URL from phyphox again and confirm both devices use the same network |
| phyphox URL works on the phone but not on the laptop | eduroam or another managed network uses client isolation | Use a phone hotspot, local router, or travel router |
| Dashboard stops receiving samples | Phone went asleep or phyphox stopped running in the foreground | Keep the phone awake and leave the phyphox experiment open |
| Dashboard reports remote access or connection errors | phyphox remote access is off | Enable remote access in phyphox and paste the shown URL into Streamlit |
| Model artifact error | Required files are missing from `models/` | Regenerate or restore the model artifacts before the demo |
| Feature mismatch error | Live Feature Vector does not match the saved training feature columns | Use the project feature extraction pipeline and do not manually edit feature names |
| Predictions appear slowly | Window size is long | Use a smaller Window for a more responsive classroom demo |
| Predictions look unstable | Window size is short or the phone motion is inconsistent | Use a larger Window or repeat the activity more steadily |

---

## Checkpoint

Before continuing, you should be able to answer:

1. Why can eduroam block the live demo?
2. What does the live buffer store?
3. When does the dashboard start predicting?
4. Why do Predictions react with delay?
5. Why must live feature extraction match training feature extraction?

---

## Further Exploration

Optional: compare predictions with two different window sizes.

How does a smaller window affect responsiveness?

How does a larger window affect stability?

---

## What Have We Achieved?

- We connected phyphox Sensor Stream data to the project pipeline.
- We traced the live path from `LiveBuffer` to Window to Feature Vector to Prediction.
- We explained why eduroam and other managed networks can block the demo.
- We interpreted dashboard states as debugging signals.
- We prepared a troubleshooting checklist for reliable Live Prediction.

## What Comes Next?

You have now followed the full system from raw Sensor Stream to Live Prediction. The remaining project materials help you review, teach, and verify the complete workbook.
